title: AP World History: Modern DBQ Practice Exam 11

questions.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which European imperial economic policies transformed Southeast Asian societies from c. 1830 to 1900.
Document 1
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Report from a Dutch colonial administrator to officials in Batavia, 1835, Java.
Source Type: Colonial administrative report.
“The cultivation system has increased delivery of coffee and sugar to government warehouses. Village heads are responsible for assigning land and labor, and the treasury benefits from predictable production. Some districts complain of hunger when too much attention is given to export crops, but the system teaches discipline and makes the colony useful to the kingdom.”
Sourcing cues: Colonial official defending state-directed export production; emphasizes revenue and order while minimizing local hardship.
Document 2
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Petition from Javanese village elders to a district official, 1848, Central Java.
Source Type: Local petition.
“Our wet-rice fields have long fed our households, paid local obligations, and supported ceremonies. Now laborers are called away to plant cane and carry loads for the government crop. When rains fail, the village has little reserve. We ask that rice fields not be taken at the season when food must be secured.”
Sourcing cues: Local village perspective; emphasizes subsistence, labor burdens, and disruption caused by export-crop demands.
Document 3
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Export summary from a British commercial office, 1860–1890, Rangoon.
Source Type: Plain-text data table.
Selected Rice Exports from Lower Burma
Year Reported Pattern
1860 Rice exports increasing after expanded British control
1870 New canals and mills support larger shipments
1880 Delta lands brought into commercial rice production
1890 Rangoon becomes a major rice-exporting port
Note on the table: The report linked export growth to river transport, land clearing, credit, and demand from overseas markets.
Sourcing cues: British commercial perspective; highlights export growth but does not show debt, labor conditions, or ecological effects.
Document 4
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Memorandum from a French official in Cochinchina, 1882, Saigon.
Source Type: Colonial memorandum.
“Roads, canals, and schools will allow the colony to produce more efficiently and to understand French methods. Vietnamese clerks trained in our language can assist administration, but education must remain practical and loyal. The purpose is not to create critics of France but useful intermediaries for agriculture, taxation, and order.”
Sourcing cues: French colonial official; supports infrastructure and limited education to strengthen administration and production.
Document 5
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Description of a colonial infrastructure map of mainland Southeast Asia, 1895.
Source Type: Described map.
The map shows rail and river routes connecting inland rice, teak, tin, and rubber-producing districts to ports such as Rangoon, Saigon, Singapore, and Bangkok. European-controlled territories are shaded differently from Siam, which remains formally independent. Telegraph lines run beside several rail routes. A legend marks “export zones,” “customs stations,” “plantation districts,” and “mining areas.”
Sourcing cues: Colonial cartographic source; presents infrastructure as orderly economic development while highlighting export corridors and imperial boundaries.
Document 6
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Letter from a Chinese merchant in Singapore to a relative in Fujian, 1896.
Source Type: Private letter.
“Tin mines and rubber gardens bring profit to those who can supply workers, food, and credit. Men from our district arrive each month, some with contracts, some with debts, and some hoping to open shops. British law protects trade when it suits officials, but taxes, licenses, and police rules remind us that this is not our country.”
Sourcing cues: Diaspora merchant perspective; shows opportunity, migration, credit networks, and colonial regulation.
Document 7
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Editorial from a Filipino reform newspaper, 1898, Manila.
Source Type: Newspaper editorial.
“The colony has been told that roads, schools, and commerce are gifts of civilization. Yet land taxes grow, friar estates remain powerful, and local voices are treated as disorder when they ask for representation. If commerce expands but dignity is denied, the educated youth will no longer confuse export wealth with justice.”
Sourcing cues: Reformist colonial newspaper; links economic change to political criticism and educated nationalist sentiment.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 7
Thesis / Claim — 1 point: Earns the point by making a historically defensible claim that evaluates the extent to which European imperial economic policies transformed Southeast Asian societies from c. 1830 to 1900. A strong thesis should establish a line of reasoning, such as arguing that imperial policies transformed agriculture, labor, infrastructure, migration, education, and export production while preserving some local hierarchies and provoking social hardship and political criticism.
Contextualization — 1 point: Earns the point by describing a broader historical context relevant to nineteenth-century imperialism in Southeast Asia, such as industrial demand for raw materials, European maritime power, the expansion of colonial states, steamships and telegraphs, plantation agriculture, global commodity markets, missionary and educational activity, Chinese and Indian migration, or the competition among European empires.
Evidence from the Documents — 2 points: Earns 1 point by accurately using the content of at least three documents to address the prompt. Earns 2 points by using at least six documents to support an argument about export crops, village labor, commercial rice, infrastructure, education, migration, credit, colonial regulation, or reformist criticism.
Evidence Beyond the Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by using at least one specific piece of relevant outside evidence not found in the documents, such as the Dutch Cultivation System, British conquest of Burma, French Indochina, the Suez Canal, Singapore as an entrepôt, Chinese labor migration to Malaya, the Philippine Revolution, Siamese modernization under Mongkut or Chulalongkorn, or rubber and tin exports.
Sourcing / Analysis of Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by explaining how or why the point of view, purpose, audience, or historical situation of at least three documents is relevant to the argument. Effective examples include explaining why colonial officials emphasized revenue and order, why village elders focused on subsistence, why commercial tables omit social costs, why French officials limited education, why maps present export corridors as progress, why merchants emphasized opportunity and regulation, or why reform newspapers linked economic change to political demands.
Complexity — 1 point: Earns the point by demonstrating a more complex understanding of imperial economic transformation. This may include explaining that imperial policies created new infrastructure and export wealth while increasing labor burdens and dependency, that migrants and local intermediaries could benefit while rural communities suffered, or that economic change helped produce new political criticism and nationalism.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong DBQ could argue that European imperial economic policies greatly transformed Southeast Asian societies by reorganizing agriculture around export crops, expanding commercial rice and plantation production, building infrastructure, increasing migration, and strengthening colonial administration. Documents 1, 3, 4, and 5 support arguments about state-directed production, export corridors, and infrastructure. Documents 2, 6, and 7 show social costs, migrant opportunity, colonial regulation, and political criticism. Outside evidence could include the Dutch Cultivation System, British Burma, French Indochina, Singapore, the Philippine Revolution, Chinese migration to Malaya, or Siamese reforms. Strong sourcing should compare colonial administrators, village elders, commercial observers, diaspora merchants, cartographers, and reformist newspaper writers.
